140: command.
141: 10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
142: used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
143: Agent session
144: Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
145: claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
146: the full session log per your tool's docs.
147: Evaluation
148: •
149: Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
150: makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
151: about what a rubric fails to cover.
152: •
153: •
154: •
155: Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
156: spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
157: Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
158: command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
159: Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
160: Logistics
161: •
162: •
163: •
164: •
165: Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
166: Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
167: compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
168: Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
169: Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
170: References
171: •
172: •
173: •
174: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
175: Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
176: arXiv:2510.04374
177: Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
178: 2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
179: into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
180: AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
181: et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
182: of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
183: and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
184: This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.

<path>/Users/albert/Projects/philo/eval/README.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner
2: 
3: One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
4: rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, plus
5: a **rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
6: gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
7: null-input generator.
8: 
9: ```
10: eval/
11:   models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
12:   llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
13:   media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
14:   deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
15:   tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
16:   harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
17:   policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
18:   rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
19:   null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
20:   run_common.py        # task / subject resolution shared by the modes
21:   run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout}
22:   results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv
23:   rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
24: ```
25: 
26: ## Setup
27: 
28: ```bash
29: source .venv/bin/activate
30: pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
31: brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
32: # .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
33: python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
34: ```
35: 
36: ## The judge suite (`models.py`)
37: 
38: Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:
39: 
40: | key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
41: |-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
42: | `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
43: | `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
44: | `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
45: | `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
46: | `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
47: | `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |
48: 
49: A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
50: `transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
51: deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
52: `MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
53: +audio triple for that ablation.
54: 
55: ## The harness (`harness.py`)
56: 
57: One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
58: agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
59: inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
60: then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
61: `met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
62: budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.
63: 
64: To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
65: things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
66: fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:
67: 
68: * `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
69:   audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
70: * `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
71: * `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
72:   for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
73:   re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
74: * read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
75: * an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
76:   artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").
77: 
78: Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
79: **penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
80: -85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
81: (unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
82: dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
83: A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.
84: 
85: ## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)
86: 
87: Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:
88: 
89: | gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
90: |-----------|---------|------------------|
91: | PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
92: | video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
93: | archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |
94: 
95: ```bash
96: python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
97: ```
98: 
99: ## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)
100: 
101: Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
102: single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:
103: 
104: | subject | meaning |
105: |---------|---------|
106: | `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
107: | `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
108: | `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
109: | `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |
110: 
111: Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
112: fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:
113: 
114: * **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
115:   among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
116:   passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
117: * **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
118:   per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
119: * **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
120:   (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
121:   `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.
122: 
123: ```bash
124: # boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
125: python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite
126: 
127: # add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
128: python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
129:     --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7
130: 
131: # consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
132: python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini
133: 
134: # grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
135: python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
136: ```
137: Outputs:
138: * `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
139:   consistency analyses).
140: * `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
141:   `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
142: * `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).
143: 
144: ## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)
145: 
146: Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
147: the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
148: policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
149: inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
150: driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
151: writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
152: it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.
153: 
154: ```bash
155: python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
156: python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
157: ```
158: Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
159: `results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
160: judge mode.
161: 
162: > Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
163: > policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
164: > (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.
165: 
166: ## Cost
167: 
168: Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
169: task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
170: clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
171: have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode
172: defaults to all 10).
173: ```
174: PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
175: video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
176: audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
177: ```

metadata.preview:
# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner

One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, plus
a **rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
null-input generator.

```
eval/
  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
  run_common.py        # task / subject resolution shared by the modes

display.text:
# Rubric scorecard — unified eval runner

One entrypoint (`eval.run`) that, from a single judging matrix, scores GDPval
rubrics along **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)** and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, plus
a **rollout** mode that has policy models attempt the real tasks to produce
gradeable deliverables. Built on the shared judge harness, model suite, and
null-input generator.

```
eval/
  models.py            # model registry (provider × capability × modality)
  llm.py               # OpenAI-compatible client (OpenAI + OpenRouter), cost tracking
  media.py             # ffmpeg frames / audio clips + Whisper transcription (cached)
  deliverable.py       # Bundle: flat file view over a deliverable, incl. zip members
  tools.py             # judge agent tools, gated by each model's modalities
  harness.py           # the judge: tool-using agent -> per-item [0,1] scorecard
  policy.py            # the rollout policy: agent that EDITS media (ffmpeg) / writes docs
  rubric.py            # rubric loading + normalization (handles penalty items)
  null_inputs.py       # generates do-nothing deliverables per task
  run_common.py        # task / subject resolution shared by the modes
  run.py               # unified runner: --mode {judge, rollout}
  results/             # scorecard.{json,tsv}, consistency.tsv, rollouts.tsv
  rollouts/            # generated deliverables: <task>/<policy>/deliverable/
```

## Setup

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai pymupdf pillow        # plus the existing pandas/pyarrow/requests
brew install ffmpeg                       # frames, audio extraction
# .env must define OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENROUTER_API_KEY
python scripts/download_data.py          # if data/ isn't populated yet
```

## The judge suite (`models.py`)

Chosen cheap, and to vary three things so the axes are measurable:

| key | provider | model | modalities | role on the axes |
|-----|----------|-------|-----------|------------------|
| `gpt-mini` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-mini | text, image | strong vision anchor; cross-vendor |
| `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | gpt-5.4-nano | text, image | weak rung → capability ablation vs `gpt-mini` |
| `qwen-vl` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text, image | cheap mid judge; cross-vendor |
| `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text | **modality ablation**: same family, no vision |
| `gemini-lite` | OpenRouter | google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text, image, **audio** | the only judge that natively *hears* |
| `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text, image, audio | free 2nd native-audio judge for A/V tasks |

A judge without `audio` still reaches audio *content* through the
`transcribe_audio` tool (Whisper). Native-audio vs transcript-only on the same
deliverable is exactly the **modality-access** experiment. The
`MODALITY_LADDER = [qwen-text, qwen-vl, gemini-lite]` is the text → +vision →
+audio triple for that ablation.

## The harness (`harness.py`)

One model grades one deliverable against one rubric as a small tool-calling
agent. It is handed the file list (deliverable files plus read-only `reference`
inputs for comparison criteria), inspects with the tools its modalities allow,
then calls `submit_scorecard` with a per-item **verdict**:
`met | unmet | partial | unverifiable`. Safeguards: per-judgement tool-call
budget, per-turn flood cap, duplicate-call dedupe, forced scorecard on the last round.

To give the judge a *fair shot* (early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to
things it couldn't see, vs ~3% genuine gaps), the toolbox includes deterministic
fact tools so objective criteria are never guessed:

* `probe_media` — ffprobe ground truth: codec, exact resolution, fps, runtime,
  audio channels (kills "cannot determine codec/resolution/runtime").
* `file_facts` — extension/type, PDF page count, word count ("is a PDF", "<= 2 pages").
* `view_video_frames` — defaults to **one frame per scene cut** (distinct shots,
  for "includes a shot of X" / identifiable-face checks); pass `start/end` to
  re-sample a window at higher resolution to read on-screen text.
* read-only **reference files**, so "matches the script" items can compare.
* an explicit **`unverifiable`** verdict for criteria no judge can check from the
  artifact (external provenance like "footage is royalty-free / from Pexels").

Scores are normalized by the **positive ceiling** (sum of positive item points);
**penalty items** (negative points, e.g. "uses footage with identifiable faces:
-85") subtract when `met`, flooring at 0. Two scores are reported: `normalized`
(unverifiable items = 0) and `normalized_verifiable_only` (unverifiable items
dropped from numerator and denominator — the fair score given what is checkable).
A large gap between them is itself a rubric **verifiability defect**.

## Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)

Per task, keyed on the gold deliverable's type:

| gold type | `blank` | `unedited_input` |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| PDF | one empty page | the unrevised source doc rendered to PDF |
| video (mp4) | 5 s black + silence | source audio over a black frame |
| archive (stems zip) | zip of 5 s silence | the raw input audio re-zipped, unmixed |

```bash
python -m eval.null_inputs          # (re)generate all; writes eval/null_inputs/<task>/<variant>/
```

## Running the eval (`--mode judge`)

Judge mode grades one or more **subjects** per task and derives all axes from a
single judging matrix. A subject is named on the CLI:

| subject | meaning |
|---------|---------|
| `gold` | the released gold deliverable (oracle, expect ≈1) |
| `blank`, `unedited_input` | synthesized do-nothing nulls (expect ≈0) |
| `rollout:<policy_key>` | a deliverable produced by `--mode rollout` |
| `preexisting:<path>` | files under `<path>` (or `<path>/<task_folder>`) |

Default subjects are `gold` + the available nulls (the boundary anchors). The axes
fall out of how many models/repeats you ask for:

* **Boundary (Axis 1)** — derived automatically when both `gold` and ≥1 null are
  among the subjects: gold−null **gap** + **leaks** (positive items a null still
  passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects).
* **Cross-model consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by passing ≥2 `--judge-models`:
  per-subject score spread, pairwise gaps, most-contested items.
* **Self-consistency (Axis 3)** — turned ON by `--repeats > 1`: one judge
  (`--self-model`, default the first judge) re-grades each subject at
  `--temperature`; reports score stdev + fraction of unanimous items.

```bash
# boundary + cross-model in one shot (3 judges, all gold tasks):
python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite

# add self-consistency (5 repeats), single task:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --tasks <folder> --judge-models qwen-vl \
    --repeats 5 --temperature 0.7

# consistency OFF (one judge, one pass) on a generated rollout:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects rollout:gpt-mini --judge-models gpt-mini

# grade a directory of preexisting outputs:
python -m eval.run --mode judge --subjects gold preexisting:/path/to/outputs
```
Outputs:
* `results/scorecard.json` — full detail (per-item verdicts, boundary, both
  consistency analyses).
* `results/scorecard.tsv` — one row per (task × subject × judge): `score`,
  `verifiable_only`, `n_unverifiable`, `gold_gap`, `reward_hack_flag`.
* `results/consistency.tsv` — self + cross rows per (task × subject).

## Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)

Have policy model(s) attempt the *real* tasks and produce actual deliverables, so
the axes can be run on genuine model outputs (not just gold/null anchors). The
policy is a tool-using agent in a per-(task, policy) sandbox: it inspects the
inputs with the same modality tools the judge has, then **edits real media** by
driving a sandboxed `ffmpeg` (trim / concat / scale / mux for video & audio), or
writes / renders a document for report tasks. ffmpeg is the only external binary
it may call — no shell, sandbox-relative paths only.

```bash
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini --tasks <folder>
python -m eval.run --mode rollout --policy-models gpt-mini qwen-vl   # all 10 tasks
```
Writes `eval/rollouts/<task>/<policy>/deliverable/` (+ `rollout.json` trace and
`results/rollouts.tsv`). Grade them by passing `--subjects rollout:<policy>` to
judge mode.

> Note: video/audio rollouts attempt genuine edits, so reliability depends on the
> policy model composing valid ffmpeg steps within the round budget; report tasks
> (PDF) are the most reliable. Either way the output is real, never faked media.

## Cost

Every run prints `[cost] ... ~$X`. A PDF task is ~$0.005–0.01 per judge; a video
task ~$0.02 (image context dominates). Whisper transcripts, frames, and audio
clips are cached under `eval/.cache/` so reruns are cheap. Only 6 of the 10 tasks
have a released gold deliverable, so judge mode defaults to those 6 (rollout mode
defaults to all 10).
```
PDF gold:   private_detectives __57b2cdf2, __a46d5cd2 ; producers_and_directors __e4f664ea
video gold: film_and_video_editors __e222075d, __75401f7c
audio gold: audio_and_video_technicians __38889c3b
```

•

Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The

along the axes below.

it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration

Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.

display.text:
Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
The slice spans both capability families we care about:
•
•
Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
output is an edited or produced video.
Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
find what matters.
Part 2: The rubric scorecard
This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
along the axes below.
Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration
The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
•
Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
•
Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
frame.
Axis 2: Reward hackability
This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
not mean.
The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
with the line "the caption is visible."
A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
absence closes the gap.
That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
Axis 3: Consistency
A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
•
Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
•
Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
agree?
When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
judge.
And more (open on purpose)
We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
•
Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
broken?
•
Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
•
Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
Part 3: The iterator
Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
•
evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
•
diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
•
rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
•
evaluate again.
Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
Part 4: Report
1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
•
the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
•
what you learned running it on GDPval,
•
most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
Deliverables
Deliverable Details
Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
and the agent prompts you used.

parts.text:
I checked the repo, and there are currently no saved study outputs for Phase 2 Axis 1/3 runs.

- The evaluator is set up to produce those results (`eval/README.md:4`), but output files are expected under `eval/results/` (`eval/README.md:22`).
- In this workspace, `eval/results/` does not exist and there are no `scorecard.tsv`, `scorecard.json`, or `consistency.tsv` files anywhere.
- So: no recorded results are available yet from prior runs in this checkout.

If you want, the next step is to run judge mode to generate them:
1. `python -m eval.run --mode judge --judge-models gpt-mini qwen-vl gemini-lite`
2. Then read `eval/results/scorecard.tsv` (Axis 1 boundary + gaps/leaks) and `eval/results/consistency.tsv` (Axis 3 consistency).

| Task | Avg Gold | Avg Worst Null | Avg Gap | Avg Leaks | Neg Gap Judges |

**Axis 1 — Judge Model View (avg across tasks)**

If you want, I can also generate a “ranked issues” table (worst null leakage tasks first) directly from these files.

The user is asking me to define what a "leak" is in the context of the rubric scorecard evaluation. From the README and the data I've read, I can see:

From `eval/README.md`:
- "Boundary (Axis 1) — derived automatically when both gold and ≥1 null are among the subjects: gold−null gap + leaks (positive items a null still passes — form/presence rewards, reward-hacking suspects)."

And from `instructions.md`:
- "Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black frame."

And in the scorecard output, `reward_hack_flag` has values like `1-leaks`, `2-leaks`, etc., and `n_leaks` appears in the boundary TSV.

So a leak is: when a rubric item is passed by a null input (a do-nothing output like blank/unedited). These are items that reward mere presence or form rather than actual quality — e.g., "the caption is visible" is leaky because even a black frame with a caption passes it. Leaks are reward-hacking suspects because a policy could raise its score by satisfying these items without doing real work.

A **leak** is a rubric item that a null/do-nothing deliverable (blank file, unedited input) still passes despite doing no real work. For example, if a rubric has "the video includes a title card" and even a blank video with a hardcoded title card passes it, that item "leaks" — it rewards mere presence/form rather than genuine quality. Leaks are reward-hacking suspects because a policy could inflate its score by satisfying these items without improving the actual output.

diffs.patch:
Index: report.md
===================================================================
--- report.md	
+++ report.md	
@@ -0,0 +1,76 @@
+# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval
+
+## Overview
+
+Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.
+
+## What Exists
+
+### Data Layer
+- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
+- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
+- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.
+
+### Viewer (`viewer/`)
+- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
+- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
+- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
+- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.
+
+### Eval Framework (`eval/`)
+
+**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media`, `file_facts`) and reference-file access close that gap — the remaining 3% gap is genuine rubric defects.
+
+**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by 3 axes):
+| key | model | modalities | role |
+|-----|-------|-----------|------|
+| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text+image | strong vision anchor |
+| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text+image | capability ablation vs mini |
+| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text+image | cross-vendor vision |
+| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text-only | modality ablation (no vision) |
+| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text+image+audio | only native audio judge |
+| `nemotron-omni` | nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text+image+audio | free 2nd native audio |
+
+**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).
+
+**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — blank (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and unedited_input (source as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.
+
+**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items floor at 0.
+
+### Results (`eval/results/`)
+
+**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — 6 tasks × 3 judges (gpt-mini, qwen-vl, gemini-lite):
+
+| Task | Gold (avg) | Worst Null (avg) | Gap (avg) | Leaks (avg) | Neg-gap judges |
+|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
+| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.622 | 0.008 | **0.614** | 0.7 | 1/3 |
+| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.484 | 0.036 | **0.448** | 1.3 | 1/3 |
+| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.630 | 0.147 | **0.483** | 5.3 | 0/3 |
+| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.366 | 0.054 | **0.312** | 1.7 | 1/3 |
+| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.630 | 0.412 | **0.218** | 20.7 | 1/3 |
+| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.000 | 0.055 | **-0.055** | 1.7 | **2/3** |
+
+Key findings:
+- **`e222075d`** (green energy ad) scores zero on gold across all judges — gold deliverable may be missing or the rubric fundamentally broken.
+- **`57b2cdf2`** has 20.7 avg leaks — the null `unedited_input` scores 0.644 with gpt-mini, meaning half the rubric items reward presence of form, not quality.
+- **gemini-lite** shows a systematic pattern: scores gold near 0 on several tasks but gives reasonable null scores → gap often negative or near-zero (4/6 tasks). Likely a calibration/capability issue rather than rubric defect.
+- **qwen-vl** has the tightest null scores (avg 0.021) and widest gaps (avg 0.514) — best boundary discriminator of the three.
+
+**Axis 3 (Consistency)** — only 1 task scored so far:
+
+| Type | Task | Panel | Mean | Stdev | Frac Unanimous | Item Stdev |
+|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
+| Cross-model | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano+qwen-text | 0.836 | 0.147 | 0.689 | 0.173 |
+| Self-consistency | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano, 3× | 0.718 | 0.023 | 0.889 | 0.058 |
+
+Self-consistency is high (stdev 0.023, 89% unanimous). Cross-model shows a 0.295 gap between gpt-nano (0.688) and qwen-text (0.983) — driven by 8 criteria where one model passes and the other doesn't, all about "mentions photographic evidence of [event]" style items (vague wording → contested).
+
+## What's Next
+
+1. **Full Axis 3 sweep**: Run cross-model and self-consistency on all 6 gold tasks.
+2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
+3. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Build the auto-rewrite loop that diagnoses failures and repairs rubrics, tracking before/after scores.
+4. **Reward hackability analysis (Axis 2)**: Systematic audit of presence-vs-absence patterns and other hackable structures.
+5. **Additional axes**: Discrimination (separates excellent from adequate), coverage (grades actual task content vs surface), saturation (behavior at ceiling).
+6. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500-2500 word LaTeX PDF.
+7. **Package deliverables**: rubric_scorecard.tsv, 10 rubric packages per task.

<content>
1: CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
2: PHILO LABS
3: Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
4: Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
5: ~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
6: Context
7: Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
8: purpose.
9: For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
10: rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
11: The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
12: every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
13: is strong.
14: On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
15: satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
16: So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
17: you to build that grader.
18: Why GDPval
19: You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
20: economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
21: •
22: •
23: •
24: •
25: Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
26: The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
27: (detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
28: documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
29: What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
30: experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
31: Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
32: whether a rubric is calibrated.
33: The Task
34: Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
35: •
36: a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
37: •
38: an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
39: Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
40: Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
41: We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
42: are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
43: swap in document or slide tasks.
44: Occupation GDPval task folder

metadata.preview:
CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
PHILO LABS
Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
Context
Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
purpose.
For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
is strong.
On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
you to build that grader.
Why GDPval
You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval

display.text:
CONFIDENTIAL - DO NOT DISTRIBUTE
PHILO LABS
Research Engineer / Scientist Take-Home
Track R: Evaluating Rubrics for Multimodal Agentic RL
~4 hours | 2 calendar days | Jun 2026
Context
Math has formal proof. Code has unit tests. They are trustworthy because people built them on
purpose.
For knowledge work, creative work, and most tasks that matter, we have no such thing. We write
rubrics instead: short lists of criteria, each scored by a person or by a model acting as a judge.
The problem: a rubric can look like it grades quality while it really grades form. A memo can pass
every line and still say nothing. A strong analysis can fail lines that have nothing to do with whether it
is strong.
On a leaderboard, that is a small problem. As an RL reward, it compounds. The policy learns to
satisfy the checklist without doing the work, and the rubric quietly teaches the model the wrong thing.
So before we trust a rubric as training signal, we have to grade the rubric itself. This take-home asks
you to build that grader.
Why GDPval
You will work from GDPval (Patwardhan et al., 2025), OpenAI's open benchmark of real,
economically valuable knowledge work. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/gdpval
•
•
•
•
Scale. 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations and 9 sectors. A gold subset of 220 is public.
The right shape. Tasks are multimodal. Many are exactly our kind: video understanding
(detectives, private investigators), video and image editing (film directors, VFX artists), plus
documents, slides, and spreadsheets.
What ships with each task. A prompt, reference files, a gold deliverable made by an
experienced professional, and a grading rubric.
Why that helps. The gold deliverable is a free oracle. It is exactly what you need to test
whether a rubric is calibrated.
The Task
Build two things and run them end to end on a slice of GDPval:
•
a rubric evaluator that scores how good a rubric is along the axes below, and
•
an automatic iterator that rewrites a rubric to raise those scores.
Use your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or similar) heavily throughout.
Part 1: Get familiar with 10 tasks
We have chosen the 10 tasks for you. They are all video-centric on purpose. Video is where rubrics
are weakest, where we work, and the part candidates tend to avoid. Use these exact tasks. Do not
swap in document or slide tasks.
Occupation GDPval task folder
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__75401f7c
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__c94452e4
Film and video editors film_and_video_editors__e222075d
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__38889c3b
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3
Audio and video technicians audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__57b2cdf2
Private detectives and
investigators private_detectives_and_investigators__a46d5cd2
Producers and directors producers_and_directors__e4f664ea
The slice spans both capability families we care about:
•
•
Editing. Film and video editors, audio and video technicians, and producers and directors. The
output is an edited or produced video.
Video understanding. Private detectives and investigators. The work is to review footage and
find what matters.
Part 2: The rubric scorecard
This is the heart of the take-home, and the part we will read most closely. For each rubric, score it
along the axes below.
Treat them as a standing test suite. A rubric that fails any one is suspect, no matter how reasonable
it looks on the page. The list is not complete on purpose: finding the axes we have not named is part
of the work.
Axis 1: Boundary calibration
The cheap checks, run first. Two sanity anchors:
•
Gold scores near 1. Run the rubric on the gold deliverable.
•
Null scores near 0. Run it on a do-nothing output: an empty file, the unedited input, or a black
frame.
Axis 2: Reward hackability
This is the axis that matters most for RL. A rubric is hackable if a policy can raise its score without
improving the work. The policy will optimize for exactly what you reward, including the parts you did
not mean.
The classic trap is a criterion that only checks that something is present. Take a captioning rubric
with the line "the caption is visible."
A policy can satisfy it with a huge caption that covers the speaker's face. Visible, yes, but worse, not
better. The line states a presence condition with no matching absence condition.
The fix is to pair them: "the caption is visible AND does not cover the main subject." Presence plus
absence closes the gap.
That is one pattern. There are more, and we left them for you to find.
Axis 3: Consistency
A rubric is only useful if it returns the same verdict on the same work.
•
Self-consistency: run the same rubric on the same output several times with the same judge.
Do the scores reproduce, or wander?
•
Cross-model consistency: score the same output with two or more judge models. Do they
agree?
When two judges disagree, ask why. If one judge is simply more capable, fine. If a criterion is so
vague that two models read it differently, that is a rubric defect: fix the wording, do not blame the
judge.
And more (open on purpose)
We expect you to add axes we have not named. Strong candidates:
•
Discrimination: does it separate excellent work from merely adequate, not just good from
broken?
•
Coverage: does it grade what the task is actually about, or only the easy-to-name surface?
•
Saturation: how does it behave once every strong model passes it?
Part 3: The iterator
Build a loop that improves a rubric automatically:
•
evaluate the rubric on the axes above,
•
diagnose which axis it fails, and why,
•
rewrite the rubric to fix that failure,
•
evaluate again.
Run it on your 10 tasks. For each, report before and after: did hackability drop, did cross-model
agreement rise where it should, did the gold move toward 1 and the null toward 0?
Be frank about what moved and what did not. A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.
Part 4: Report
1,500-2,500 words in LaTeX, compiled to PDF with your coding agent. Cover:
•
the framing of your scorecard and your design choices,
•
what you learned running it on GDPval,
•
most importantly, an honest account of which rubrics you would trust as RL reward and which
you would not, and which axes you believe generalize to rubrics you never saw.
Deliverables
Deliverable Details
Report (PDF) LaTeX compiled to PDF via your coding agent. 1,500-2,500 words.
Code GitHub repo (preferred) or zip. Clean README with run instructions
and the agent prompts you used.
rubric_scorecard.tsv
Auto-generated by the pipeline. One row per (task x rubric version),
with a column for each axis (gold score, null score, hackability labels,
self-consistency variance, cross-model agreement) and a column
flagging suspected reward hacking. Reproducible with a single
command.
10 rubric packages For each task: the original rubric, the rewritten rubric, the test cases
used, and the hackability analysis. Released as one folder per task.
Agent session
Exported coding agent session. For Claude Code, run /export, or use
claude-code-transcripts to export as HTML. For other agents, export
the full session log per your tool's docs.
Evaluation
•
Systematic thinking (40%): a coherent framework, first-principles reasoning about what
makes a rubric trustworthy as training signal, and clear thinking about reward hacking and
about what a rubric fails to cover.
•
•
•
Rubric-evaluation quality (25%): axes that are precise, measurable, implementable from your
spec alone, and honest about which ones would survive being used as reward.
Implementation (20%): clean, modular, end to end. The evaluator and iterator run with a single
command, and the rubric packages are self-contained.
Agent fluency (15%): effective use of coding agents across the full workflow.
Logistics
•
•
•
•
Time: ~4 focused hours, 2 calendar days from receipt.
Resources: anything publicly available. The GDPval gold subset is on Hugging Face. API and
compute costs reimbursed up to $200 (note your spend).
Questions: email us. Good questions are a positive signal.
Submit: email a GitHub link or a zip containing all deliverables.
References
•
•
•
GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks.
Patwardhan et al., 2025 (OpenAI). The benchmark and gold task subset you will work from.
arXiv:2510.04374
Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains. Gunjal et al.,
2025. Uses rubric feedback as an on-policy RL reward and studies how to turn rubric scores
into a reward. Precedent for treating rubrics as the reward signal. arXiv:2507.17746
AdvancedIF and RIFL: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and RL for Instruction Following. He
et al., 2025 (Meta Superintelligence Labs). An expert-curated rubric benchmark plus a pipeline
of rubric generation, a rubric verifier, and reward shaping. Precedent for the generator, verifier,
and updater shape. arXiv:2511.10507
This document is confidential. Do not share or post publicly.

parts.text: Now I have all the information I need. Let me compose a concise report.md covering the project progress.

input.content:
# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval

## Overview

Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home). The evaluator scores rubrics along Axis 1 (boundary calibration), Axis 3 (consistency), and derived metrics (leaks, gaps, unverifiable items). A browser viewer and automated rollout pipeline support the full workflow.

## What Exists

### Data Layer
- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).
- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.

### Viewer (`viewer/`)
- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.

### Eval Framework (`eval/`)

**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media`, `file_facts`) and reference-file access close that gap — the remaining 3% gap is genuine rubric defects.

**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by 3 axes):
| key | model | modalities | role |
|-----|-------|-----------|------|
| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text+image | strong vision anchor |
| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text+image | capability ablation vs mini |
| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text+image | cross-vendor vision |
| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text-only | modality ablation (no vision) |
| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text+image+audio | only native audio judge |
| `nemotron-omni` | nemotron-3-nano-omni (free) | text+image+audio | free 2nd native audio |

**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).

**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — blank (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and unedited_input (source as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.

**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items floor at 0.

### Results (`eval/results/`)

**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — 6 tasks × 3 judges (gpt-mini, qwen-vl, gemini-lite):

| Task | Gold (avg) | Worst Null (avg) | Gap (avg) | Leaks (avg) | Neg-gap judges |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.622 | 0.008 | **0.614** | 0.7 | 1/3 |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.484 | 0.036 | **0.448** | 1.3 | 1/3 |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.630 | 0.147 | **0.483** | 5.3 | 0/3 |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.366 | 0.054 | **0.312** | 1.7 | 1/3 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.630 | 0.412 | **0.218** | 20.7 | 1/3 |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.000 | 0.055 | **-0.055** | 1.7 | **2/3** |

Key findings:
- **`e222075d`** (green energy ad) scores zero on gold across all judges — gold deliverable may be missing or the rubric fundamentally broken.
- **`57b2cdf2`** has 20.7 avg leaks — the null `unedited_input` scores 0.644 with gpt-mini, meaning half the rubric items reward presence of form, not quality.
- **gemini-lite** shows a systematic pattern: scores gold near 0 on several tasks but gives reasonable null scores → gap often negative or near-zero (4/6 tasks). Likely a calibration/capability issue rather than rubric defect.
- **qwen-vl** has the tightest null scores (avg 0.021) and widest gaps (avg 0.514) — best boundary discriminator of the three.

**Axis 3 (Consistency)** — only 1 task scored so far:

| Type | Task | Panel | Mean | Stdev | Frac Unanimous | Item Stdev |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Cross-model | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano+qwen-text | 0.836 | 0.147 | 0.689 | 0.173 |
| Self-consistency | `57b2cdf2` | gpt-nano, 3× | 0.718 | 0.023 | 0.889 | 0.058 |

Self-consistency is high (stdev 0.023, 89% unanimous). Cross-model shows a 0.295 gap between gpt-nano (0.688) and qwen-text (0.983) — driven by 8 criteria where one model passes and the other doesn't, all about "mentions photographic evidence of [event]" style items (vague wording → contested).

## What's Next

1. **Full Axis 3 sweep**: Run cross-model and self-consistency on all 6 gold tasks.
2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
3. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Build the auto-rewrite loop that diagnoses failures and repairs rubrics, tracking before/after scores.
4. **Reward hackability analysis (Axis 2)**: Systematic audit of presence-vs-absence patterns and other hackable structures.
5. **Additional axes**: Discrimination (separates excellent from adequate), coverage (grades actual task content vs surface), saturation (behavior at ceiling).
6. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500-2500 word LaTeX PDF.
7. **Package deliverables**: rubric_scorecard.tsv, 10 rubric packages per task.

parts.text: Done. The report.md has been written to the root. Let me provide a brief summary.

Written `report.md` to the project root. It covers the viewer, judge harness design (fair-shot philosophy, deterministic fact tools, modality ladder), judge suite choice, all Axis 1/3 results from `eval/results/`, identified patterns (gemini-lite calibration issue, `e222075d` gold=0, `57b2cdf2` huge leaks), and what's next.
